content:
# Luke Chapter 21 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 114-115 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_021_page_001.jpg, LUK_021_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And having looked up, he saw the rich casting their gifts into the treasury.

**2** And he saw a certain destitute widow casting in two small copper coins,

**3** and he said, "Truly I say to you that this poor widow put in more than all of them;

**4** for all these out of their abundance cast in unto the gifts, but she out of her want cast in all the livelihood that she had."

**5** And as some were speaking concerning the temple, that it was adorned with beautiful stones and dedicated offerings, he said,

**6** "These things which you behold — days will come in which there shall not be left stone upon stone which shall not be thrown down."

**7** And they questioned him, saying, "Teacher, when therefore shall these things be? And what is the sign when these things are about to come to pass?"

**8** And he said, "See that you be not led astray; for many shall come in my name, saying, 'I am he,' and, 'The time has drawn near.' Do not go after them.

**9** And when you hear of wars and tumults, be not terrified; for these things must come to pass first, but the end is not immediately."

**10** Then he said to them, "Nation shall rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom;

**11** and there shall be great earthquakes, and in various places famines and pestilences, and there shall be terrors and great signs from heaven.

**12** But before all these things, they shall lay their hands upon you and shall persecute you, delivering you up to the synagogues and prisons, being brought before kings and governors for the sake of my name.

**13** It shall result for you as a testimony.

**14** Set it therefore in your hearts not to prepare beforehand to make a defense;

**15** for I will give you a mouth and wisdom which all those who oppose you shall not be able to withstand or to contradict.

**16** And you shall be delivered up even by parents and brothers and kinsmen and friends, and they shall put to death some from among you,

**17** and you shall be hated by all on account of my name.

**18** And not a hair of your head shall by any means perish.

**19** In your endurance you shall gain your souls.

**20** And when you see Jerusalem being surrounded by armies, then know that her desolation has drawn near.

**21** Then let those in Judea flee to the mountains, and let those in the midst of her depart, and let not those in the country places enter into her;

**22** for these are days of vengeance, that all things that are written may be fulfilled.

**23** Woe to those who are with child and to those who nurse in those days! For there shall be great distress upon the land and wrath upon this people.

**24** And they shall fall by the edge of the sword, and shall be led captive into all the nations; and Jerusalem shall be trampled by the nations, until the times of the nations be fulfilled.

**25** And there shall be signs in sun and moon and stars, and upon the earth distress of nations in perplexity at the roaring of the sea and the surging,

**26** men fainting from fear and expectation of the things coming upon the inhabited world; for the powers of the heavens shall be shaken.

**27** And then they shall see the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory.

**28** And when these things begin to come to pass, stand upright and lift up your heads, because your redemption draws near."

**29** And he spoke a parable to them: "Look at the fig tree and all the trees;

**30** when they now put forth leaves, you see for yourselves and know that the summer is already near.

**31** So also you, when you see these things coming to pass, know that the kingdom of God is near.

**32** Truly I say to you that this generation shall by no means pass away until all things come to pass.

**33** Heaven and earth shall pass away, but my words shall by no means pass away.

**34** But take heed to yourselves, lest your hearts be weighed down with dissipation and drunkenness and the cares of this life, and that day come upon you suddenly as a snare;

**35** for it shall come upon all who dwell upon the face of all the earth.

**36** But be watchful at every season, praying that you may have strength to escape all these things that are about to come to pass, and to stand before the Son of Man."

**37** And he was teaching by day in the temple, but by night he went out and lodged on the mount that is called Olivet.

**38** And all the people came early in the morning to him in the temple to hear him.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

I examined both pages of Codex Sinaiticus covering Luke 21 (pages 114-115). The manuscript is written in the typical four-column format of Sinaiticus, in continuous uncial script. Page 1 begins at approximately Luke 20:34 and continues through 21:15, with the column at far right (column 4) carrying the text into the persecution discourse. Page 2 picks up at approximately 21:24 and continues through the chapter's end and into Luke 22. The text is generally legible and consistent with the readings reported by all three translators. No significant scribal corrections or variant readings at odds with the translators' reports were observed. All three translators correctly identified the singular NEPHELH ("cloud") in verse 27 and the imperative KTHSASQE in verse 19, which are distinctive Sinaiticus readings.

### Translation Principles Applied

This consensus translation follows a **formal-equivalent** approach, staying close to the Greek word order and syntax while ensuring clarity in English. Where all three translators agree, I adopt the shared reading without alteration. Where they diverge, I weigh the following factors: (1) fidelity to the Greek, (2) readability, and (3) whether a rendering obscures or clarifies the meaning of the source text.

### Verse-by-Verse Council Decisions on Divergences

**Verse 1:** All three translators agree on the core rendering. EMET uses "the rich [ones]"; LOGOS has "the rich"; PNEUMA paraphrases as "wealthy people." I follow LOGOS's clean "the rich," which mirrors the Greek substantive adjective PLOUSIOUS without adding brackets or paraphrase.

**Verse 2:** EMET renders PENICHRAN as "poor," LOGOS as "poor," and PNEUMA as "destitute." The Greek PENICHROS is stronger than PTOCHOS (which appears in v. 3); it denotes someone at the edge of subsistence. PNEUMA's "destitute" captures this better. I adopt "destitute" here. For LEPTA DUO, EMET has "two small-coins," LOGOS has "two small copper coins," and PNEUMA has "two tiny copper coins." I follow LOGOS: "small copper coins" is accurate and avoids the unnecessary "tiny."

**Verse 3:** EMET: "Truly I say to you that this poor widow cast-in more [than] all." LOGOS: "Truly I say to you that this poor widow cast in more than all of them." PNEUMA: "I tell you the truth: this poor widow has put in more than all of them." I follow LOGOS, which renders the Greek faithfully and adds "of them" for natural English while retaining the formal register. PNEUMA's "I tell you the truth" is idiomatic but loses the Lukan formula. I also adopt "put in" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) over EMET's hyphenated "cast-in."

**Verse 4:** EMET: "out-of their abundance cast-in into the gifts, but this [woman] out-of her lack cast-in all the living [means] which she was-having." LOGOS: "out of their abundance cast in unto the gifts, but she out of her want cast in all the livelihood that she had." PNEUMA: "gave their gifts out of their surplus, but she, out of her poverty, put in everything she had to live on." I follow LOGOS. "Livelihood" is the best single-word rendering of BIOS in this context. "Want" for HYSTEREMA is more precise than PNEUMA's "poverty" (which could suggest PTOCHEIA). EMET's "living [means]" is accurate but cumbersome.

**Verse 5:** EMET: "And [while] some were speaking about the temple..." LOGOS: "And as some were speaking concerning the temple..." PNEUMA: "When some of his disciples were remarking about the temple..." PNEUMA adds "his disciples," which is not in the Greek (TINON, "some" — unidentified speakers). I follow LOGOS's faithful rendering.

**Verse 6:** EMET: "These [things] which you are-beholding — days will come in which [there] will not be left stone upon stone which will not be thrown-down." LOGOS: "These things which you behold — days will come in which there shall not be left stone upon stone which shall not be thrown down." PNEUMA: "As for these things you are admiring, the time is coming when not one stone will be left on another. Every one of them will be torn down." I follow LOGOS. PNEUMA splits the sentence and paraphrases freely ("torn down" for KATALUTHSETAI). EMET's brackets are unnecessary. LOGOS preserves the structure and gravity of the Greek.

**Verse 7:** Minor differences. EMET: "come-to-be"; LOGOS: "come to pass"; PNEUMA: "take place." I follow LOGOS. "Come to pass" is a dignified English rendering of GINOMAI that has strong precedent in translation tradition.

**Verse 8:** EMET: "See [that] you are not led-astray... 'I am [he]'... Do not go after them." LOGOS: "See that you be not led astray... 'I am he'... Do not go after them." PNEUMA: "Be careful not to be deceived... 'I am the one!'... Do not follow them." I follow LOGOS. "Led astray" captures PLANAO better than "deceived" (which would be APATAO). PNEUMA's "I am the one!" adds an interpretive gloss absent from the Greek EGO EIMI. LOGOS preserves the ambiguity of the divine self-identification formula.

**Verse 9:** EMET: "wars and disorders"; LOGOS: "wars and tumults"; PNEUMA: "wars and uprisings." The Greek AKATASTASIAS means "instabilities/disorders/tumults." I follow LOGOS's "tumults," which captures the sense of civic upheaval without narrowing to "uprisings" (which implies organized revolt) or the flatter "disorders."

**Verse 10:** EMET: "he was saying to them" (imperfect); LOGOS: "he said to them" (aorist sense); PNEUMA: "he told them." The Greek ELEGEN is imperfect. However, both LOGOS and PNEUMA simplify to a past-tense rendering, which is standard in translation. I follow LOGOS.

**Verse 11:** All three are closely aligned. PNEUMA uses "plagues" for LOIMOI and "powerful signs from the sky" for SEMEIA MEGALA AP OURANOU. I follow LOGOS/EMET's "pestilences" (more literal for LOIMOI) and "great signs from heaven" (OURANOU standard as "heaven" in biblical register).

**Verse 12:** EMET: "they will lay their hands upon you and will persecute [you], handing-over [you] into the synagogues and prisons, [you] being led-away before kings and governors on-account-of my name." LOGOS: "they shall lay their hands upon you and shall persecute you, delivering you up to the synagogues and prisons, being brought before kings and governors for the sake of my name." PNEUMA: "people will seize you and persecute you. They will hand you over to synagogues and prisons, and you will be brought before kings and governors because of my name." I follow LOGOS. PNEUMA breaks the sentence structure and adds "people" as subject (not explicit in Greek). EMET's brackets clutter the text.

**Verse 13:** EMET: "It will result for you unto a testimony." LOGOS: "It shall result for you as a testimony." PNEUMA: "This will be your opportunity to testify." PNEUMA paraphrases freely, adding "opportunity" which is interpretive. I follow LOGOS; "as a testimony" renders EIS MARTYRION naturally.

**Verse 14:** EMET: "Place [it] therefore in your hearts not to practice-beforehand to make-a-defense." LOGOS: "Set it therefore in your hearts not to practice beforehand to make a defense." PNEUMA: "So make up your minds not to prepare your defense in advance." I follow LOGOS, but simplify "practice beforehand" to "prepare beforehand" — the Greek PROMELETAO means "to rehearse in advance," and "prepare beforehand" captures this without the awkwardness of "practice" (which could suggest physical rehearsal). PNEUMA's "make up your minds" is too colloquial for THETE EN TAIS KARDIAIS.

**Verse 15:** EMET: "to withstand or to speak-against"; LOGOS: "to withstand or to contradict"; PNEUMA: "to resist or contradict." The Greek is ANTISTENAI E ANTEIPEN. I follow LOGOS. "Contradict" better renders ANTEIPEN than "speak-against."

**Verse 16:** EMET: "relatives"; LOGOS: "kinsmen"; PNEUMA: "relatives." SYNGENON means "blood relations" beyond the immediate family. I follow LOGOS's "kinsmen," which captures the formal register and the kinship bond. EMET: "handed-over"; LOGOS: "delivered up"; PNEUMA: "betrayed." I follow LOGOS. "Delivered up" renders PARADOTHESESTHE accurately; "betrayed" is interpretive (though contextually apt).

**Verse 18:** EMET: "And a hair from your head will certainly not perish." LOGOS: "And not a hair of your head shall by any means perish." PNEUMA: "But not a single hair on your head will be lost." I follow LOGOS. "By any means" captures the emphatic double negative OU ME. PNEUMA's "lost" weakens APOLETAI ("perish/be destroyed").

**Verse 19:** All three agree on "endurance" for HYPOMONE. EMET: "you will gain your souls." LOGOS: "you shall gain your souls." PNEUMA: "you will win your lives." I follow LOGOS. "Souls" is the standard rendering of PSYCHAS in this theological context, preserving the ambiguity between "life" and "soul" that is central to the verse's meaning.

**Verse 20:** EMET: "encampments"; LOGOS: "armies"; PNEUMA: "armies." The Greek STRATOPEDON means literally "army camp/encampment" but by metonymy refers to the armies themselves. Both LOGOS and PNEUMA have "armies," which is clearer and captures the intended meaning. I follow the majority.

**Verse 21:** EMET: "let those in the regions enter into her"; LOGOS: "let not those in the country places enter into her"; PNEUMA: "those in the countryside must not enter the city." I follow LOGOS. "Country places" renders CHORAIS well. PNEUMA replaces "her" with "the city," losing the personification of Jerusalem that is present in the Greek AUTEN.

**Verse 22:** EMET: "these are days of vengeance, [for] all the [things] having been written to be fulfilled." LOGOS: "for these are days of vengeance, that all things that are written may be fulfilled." PNEUMA: "For these are days of punishment, when everything that has been written will be fulfilled." I follow LOGOS. "Vengeance" correctly renders EKDIKESEOS; PNEUMA's "punishment" narrows the meaning (EKDIKESIS includes vindication and divine justice, not merely penalty). LOGOS's purpose clause ("that...may be fulfilled") accurately renders the Greek articular infinitive.

**Verse 23:** EMET: "Woe to those having [a child] in [the] womb and to those nursing in those days." LOGOS: "Woe to those who are with child and to those who give suck in those days!" PNEUMA: "How terrible it will be for women who are pregnant or nursing babies in those days!" I follow LOGOS. The traditional "Woe" preserves the Greek OUAI. PNEUMA's "How terrible" is interpretive and loses the prophetic register. LOGOS's "who give suck" is archaic; I adjust to "who nurse" — a minor update that preserves formality while improving clarity.

**Verse 24:** EMET: "by [the] mouth of [the] sword"; LOGOS: "by the edge of the sword"; PNEUMA: "by the edge of the sword." I follow the LOGOS/PNEUMA majority with "edge of the sword," which renders the Semitism STOMATI MACHAIRES in its established English form. EMET: "trampled by [the] nations"; LOGOS: "trampled by the nations"; PNEUMA: "trampled by the Gentiles." ETHNON can mean either "nations" or "Gentiles." I follow LOGOS with "nations," which preserves the word's range and connects to "times of the nations" in the same verse.

**Verse 25:** All three are closely aligned. EMET: "[at the] sound of [the] sea and [its] surging"; LOGOS: "at the roaring of the sea and the surging"; PNEUMA: "by the roaring and surging of the sea." I follow LOGOS. "Roaring" better captures ECHOS than EMET's "sound."

**Verse 26:** All three agree substantially. EMET: "the inhabited-world"; LOGOS: "the inhabited world"; PNEUMA: "the world." I follow LOGOS. The full phrase "inhabited world" renders OIKOUMENE more precisely than PNEUMA's simple "world" (which could suggest KOSMOS).

**Verse 27:** All three agree. No divergence requiring adjudication.

**Verse 28:** EMET: "stand-upright and lift-up your heads, because your redemption draws-near." LOGOS: "stand upright and lift up your heads, because your redemption draws near." PNEUMA: "stand up straight and lift your heads, because your deliverance is drawing near." I follow LOGOS. "Redemption" (APOLYTROSIS) is theologically richer than "deliverance," retaining the connotation of ransom. "Draws near" (present tense ENGIZEI) is more accurate than PNEUMA's periphrastic "is drawing near."

**Verse 29:** EMET: "See the fig-tree"; LOGOS: "Behold the fig tree"; PNEUMA: "Look at the fig tree." The Greek IDETE is an aorist imperative of HORAO. I adopt "Look at" — it captures the imperative force naturally. "Behold" (LOGOS) is archaic for modern readers; "See" (EMET) is adequate but flat. However, on reflection, the formal register of this translation favors a slightly elevated tone: I adopt "Look at the fig tree" which is clear and dignified.

**Verse 30:** EMET: "when they already sprout, seeing [it] for yourselves, you know that the summer is already near." LOGOS: "when they now put forth leaves, seeing it, you know of yourselves that the summer is already near." PNEUMA: "When they sprout leaves, you can see for yourselves that summer is near." I follow LOGOS with minor adjustment. "Put forth leaves" renders PROBALLOSIN well. I include "you see for yourselves and know" to capture both BLEPONTES APH HEAUTON GINOSKETE.

**Verse 31:** All three substantially agree. No significant divergence.

**Verse 32:** EMET: "this generation will certainly not pass-away until all [things] should come-to-be." LOGOS: "this generation shall not pass away until all things come to pass." PNEUMA: "this generation will certainly not pass away until all these things have taken place." I follow LOGOS, but strengthen the double negative OU ME: "shall by no means pass away," which captures the emphatic force that EMET notes but renders with "certainly."

**Verse 33:** EMET: "my words will certainly not pass-away." LOGOS: "my words shall not pass away." PNEUMA: "my words will never pass away." The Greek OU ME PARELEUSONTAI uses the strongest negation. I render "shall by no means pass away" to match the same construction in verse 32 and preserve the emphatic force. PNEUMA's "never" is natural but does not mirror the Greek construction.

**Verse 34:** EMET: "take-heed to yourselves, lest your hearts should be weighed-down in dissipation and drunkenness and cares of life." LOGOS: "take heed to yourselves, lest your hearts be weighed down with dissipation and drunkenness and the cares of this life." PNEUMA: "Be on your guard, or your hearts will be weighed down by carousing, drunkenness, and the anxieties of everyday life." I follow LOGOS. "Dissipation" renders KRAIPALH better than "carousing" (PNEUMA), as KRAIPALH specifically denotes the stupor following excess drinking. "Cares of this life" is more literal for MERIMNAIS BIOTIKAIS than "anxieties of everyday life." EMET and LOGOS agree on structure; PNEUMA restructures the sentence freely. I follow LOGOS, adding "as a snare" to close the verse (as LOGOS does), keeping the snare image connected to "suddenly."

**Verse 35:** EMET: "all those sitting upon [the] face of all the earth." LOGOS: "all those who sit upon the face of all the earth." PNEUMA: "everyone who lives on the face of the whole earth." I follow LOGOS with a minor adjustment: "all who dwell upon the face of all the earth." "Dwell" captures the sense of KATHEMENOUS (sitting/dwelling) better than the literal "sit" while being more formal than PNEUMA's "lives."

**Verse 36:** EMET: "be-watchful in every season"; LOGOS: "be watchful at every season"; PNEUMA: "Stay alert at all times." I follow LOGOS. "At every season" preserves EN PANTI KAIRO (using KAIROS, "appointed time/season," not merely CHRONOS). PNEUMA's "at all times" loses this distinction. EMET: "praying that you might have-strength to escape"; LOGOS: "praying that you may have strength to escape"; PNEUMA: "praying that you may have the strength to escape." I follow LOGOS.

**Verse 37:** EMET: "he was during the days teaching in the temple, and during the nights, going-out, he was lodging at the mount called [the mount] of Olives." LOGOS: "he was teaching by day in the temple, but by night he went out and lodged on the mount that is called Olivet." PNEUMA: "Every day Jesus taught in the temple, and every evening he went out and spent the night on the hill called the Mount of Olives." I follow LOGOS for its balance of fidelity and readability. PNEUMA adds "Jesus" (not in the Greek here) and "hill" (OROS is "mount/mountain"). EMET's word order is overly literal.

**Verse 38:** EMET: "all the people were rising-early [to come] to him in the temple to hear him." LOGOS: "all the people came early in the morning to him in the temple to hear him." PNEUMA: "all the people would get up early in the morning to come and hear him in the temple." I follow LOGOS. "Came early in the morning" renders ORTHRIZEN clearly.

---

*Signed: SOPHIA*
*In council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
